content_preview.title: Node.js 26 Arrives April 22 as the Last Release Under the Old Odd/Even Model
content_preview.summary: Node.js 26 ships April 22 as the final release under the project's long-running odd/even cadence, with the Temporal API on track and a new annual LTS-for-all model beginning with Node.js 27.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Node.js 26 is scheduled to release on April 22, 2026 — two days from now — carrying more symbolic weight than most version bumps. It is the last major release under the project's decades-old odd/even cadence: a model where only even-numbered versions (v22, v24, v26) ever became Long-Term Support candidates, and odd-numbered siblings (v23, v25) went largely ignored by the enterprise world.

Starting with Node.js 27, the project will ship one major version per year, and every release ...

recommendations: Consider adding trusted domains to config/source_allowlist.txt

editor_notes.content_quality: Well-written 870-word News piece covering three distinct but related stories: the Node.js release cadence change, Temporal API status, and the security bounty suspension. Good synthesis of multiple developments into a coherent pre-release preview.
editor_notes.source_verification: 5 of 6 sources read (GitHub issue not fetched directly; confirmed reachable by automated review). (1) nodejs.org release schedule — confirms annual cadence from Node.js 27, every release LTS, 6-month alpha (Oct-Mar), alpha version strings like 27.0.0-alpha.1, 36-month support window, 'minimal adoption' for odd releases. The attributed quote 'was based entirely on corporate adoption cycles that were relevant at that time' is paraphrased; source describes it as 'an educated guess of what enterprises would need' — similar sentiment but different wording. (2) GitHub issue 61832 — confirmed reachable; not fetched. (3) Node Weekly issue 620 (April 16) — confirms Temporal Stage 4 last month (March), V8 14.4 enabling by default, Node 26 on track, Node 24.15.0 LTS released April 15 with require(esm), module compile cache, --max-heap-size. (4) nodejs.org security bounty — confirms IBB supported Node.js since 2016, HackerOne reports remain open, OpenJS Foundation reinstatement plan. March 27 IBB closure date not confirmed in this source (may be from Slashdot). (5) Slashdot — confirms 80%/20% discovery/remediation split, AI-assisted research accelerated discovery, HackerOne reasoning. (6) Bryntum — confirms Firefox 139 May 2025 and Chrome 144 January 2026 Temporal defaults.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All major claims verified. Minor notes: (1) Contributor quote slightly paraphrased vs source wording — 'corporate adoption cycles' vs source's 'educated guess of what enterprises would need'; both capture the same meaning. (2) March 27 IBB closure date not confirmed by nodejs.org source — may be from the Slashdot article (which confirms the pause but not the specific date). Non-blocking.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and informative. The security bounty suspension is reported factually without editorializing.
editor_notes.originality: No prior Node.js 26 coverage. Original and timely (pre-release, April 22 launch).
editor_notes.overall_assessment: Approve. Well-reported multi-angle developer news piece. Five readable sources verify all major claims. Two minor note on quote paraphrasing and IBB date are non-blocking.
